title: Intro
text: Welcome to Science Radar, your weekly digest of the most surprising, frontier-pushing, and cross-disciplinary discoveries from across all of science. This week we've found papers that challenge long-held assumptions, open doors to entirely new research directions, and connect ideas across fields in ways nobody expected. Let's dive in.

title: This Week's Highlights
text: And now, a quick tour through this week's other noteworthy papers.

title: Challenges in replay detection by TDLM in post-encoding resting state
text: Challenges in replay detection by TDLM in post-encoding resting state — eLife. Using temporally delayed linear modeling (TDLM) and magnetoencephalography (MEG), we investigated whether items associated with an underlying graph structure are replayed during a post-learning resting state. In these same data, we previously provided evidence for replay during online (non-rest) mem

title: Fitness drivers of division of labor in vertebrates
text: Fitness drivers of division of labor in vertebrates — eLife. Although division of labor as a means to increase productivity is a common feature in animal social groups, most previous studies have focused almost exclusively on eusocial insects with extreme task partitioning. Empirical evidence of division of labor in vertebrates is scarce, largely because we l

title: Identification and comparison of orthologous cell types from primate embryoid bodies shows limits of marker gene transferability
text: Identification and comparison of orthologous cell types from primate embryoid bodies shows limits of marker gene transferability — eLife. The identification of cell types remains a major challenge. Even after a decade of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), reasonable cell type annotations almost always include manual non-automated steps. The identification of orthologous cell types across species complicates matters even more, but

title: Metabolomics across scales: from single cells to population studies
text:
Metabolomics across scales: from single cells to population studies — Nature News.

Nature, Published online: 08 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10277-1

This Perspective reviews the potential and the challenges of metabolomics approaches, encompassing both single-cell and population-scale metabolomics.

title: When career anxiety becomes gameplay: lessons from China’s ‘young-faculty simulator’
text:
When career anxiety becomes gameplay: lessons from China’s ‘young-faculty simulator’ — Nature News.

Nature, Published online: 08 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00224-5

A popular online game simulates life as an academic — and throws the challenges of being an early-career researcher into sharp relief.

title: Audiovisual cues must be predictable and win-paired to drive risky choice
text: Audiovisual cues must be predictable and win-paired to drive risky choice — eLife. Risky or maladaptive decision making is thought to be central to the etiology of both drug and gambling addiction. Salient audiovisual cues paired with rewarding outcomes, such as the jackpot sound on a win, can enhance disadvantageous, risky choice in both rats and humans, yet it is unclear which a

title: Genetic, clinical, and biochemical profiling of Gilbert syndrome in a Nepali cohort: High prevalence of the UGT1A1 c.-3279T>G polymorphism and correlation with hematological parameters
text:
Genetic, clinical, and biochemical profiling of Gilbert syndrome in a Nepali cohort: High prevalence of the UGT1A1 c.-3279T>G polymorphism and correlation with hematological parameters — PLOS ONE.

by Pragya Gautam Ghimire, Prasanna Ghimire, Rajan Pande

Introduction

Gilbert Syndrome (GS) is a common hereditary disorder characterized by intermittent jaundice. The pathogenesis is unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia due to reduced hepatic UDP-glucuronosyltransferase 1A1 (UGT1A1) activity. I

title: Genome-wide identification of WRKY gene family members in Populus trichocarpa and their response to biotic stresses
text:
Genome-wide identification of WRKY gene family members in Populus trichocarpa and their response to biotic stresses — PeerJ. Background
WRKY transcription factors (TFs) are key regulators of plant stress resistance. Despite their importance, the regulatory mechanisms of WRKYs in Populus trichocarpa under biotic stress remain insufficiently explored. This study presents a comprehensive bioinformatic characterization of the

title: Development and validation of an early predictive model for acute stress disorder after trauma: a clinical cohort study
text:
Development and validation of an early predictive model for acute stress disorder after trauma: a clinical cohort study — PeerJ. Background

title: Efficacy and safety of tacrolimus combined with low-dose glucocorticoids vs. high-dose glucocorticoids in initial treatment of adult-onset minimal change disease: a retrospective cohort study
text:
Efficacy and safety of tacrolimus combined with low-dose glucocorticoids vs. high-dose glucocorticoids in initial treatment of adult-onset minimal change disease: a retrospective cohort study — PeerJ. Background
The treatment of adult minimal change disease (MCD) is challenging due to the side effects of high-dose and long-term glucocorticoid therapy as well as the frequent relapsing of the disease. Clinically, there is a demand for regimens that ensure rapid response and lower relapse rates, suc

title: Effect of vitamin D supplementation on blood lipids in patients with metabolic syndrome: a meta-analysis
text:
Effect of vitamin D supplementation on blood lipids in patients with metabolic syndrome: a meta-analysis — PeerJ. Background
               Vitamin D, a fat-soluble vitamin critical for bone metabolism, has recently been implicated in metabolic homeostasis. Growing evidence suggests its potential role in modulating lipid profiles in metabolic syndrome.

title: Outro
text: That's Science Radar for this week. Every one of these papers is a reminder that the most interesting science often lives at the edges — where fields collide, where assumptions break, and where the next big question is just starting to take shape. Until next week, keep questioning everything.
